documents.title: Decompilers
documents.summary: Decompilers attempt to reconstruct high-level code from compiled binaries. They are a vital complement to disassembly, providing readable control flow, types, and program structure.

documents.title: Demangling
documents.summary: Demangling is the process of converting compiler-generated symbol names back into human-readable declarations. C++ symbol mangling encodes namespaces, classes, function signatures, and calling conventions into a compact identifier.

documents.title: Disassemblers
documents.summary: Disassemblers convert machine code back into assembly instructions. They are foundational tools for binary analysis, enabling analysts to inspect program behavior, locate functions, and identify patterns.

documents.title: DWARF
documents.summary: DWARF is a standardized debugging data format used on UNIX-like systems and many compilers. It describes types, variables, stack frames, and source mappings to support symbolic debugging and reverse engineering.

documents.title: Executable and Linkable Format (ELF)
documents.summary: ELF is the standard binary format used by Linux, BSD, and UNIX-like systems. It defines sections, segments, symbol tables, dynamic linking metadata, and debug information.

documents.title: Exception Handling Metadata
documents.summary: Exception handling metadata in PE binaries supports structured unwinding, stack frame recovery, and runtime exception dispatch. On Windows x64, the runtime function table is essential for correct stack walking and exception delivery. On x86, Structured Exception Handling (SEH) uses stack-based registration records.

documents.title: Exports
documents.summary: In PE binaries, exports define the functions and data objects a module exposes to other modules. The export table is the contract a DLL or executable offers to callers, and it is one of the most valuable artifacts for reverse engineering, plug-in analysis, driver inspection, and security architecture mapping.

documents.title: Imports
documents.summary: In Windows PE binaries, imports define the external functions and data objects that a module requires from other DLLs. The import system is the loader’s contract for dependency resolution and it is a foundational artifact for reverse engineering, vulnerability analysis, and runtime behavior mapping.

documents.title: Mach-O
documents.summary: Mach-O is the native binary format for macOS and iOS. It supports fat binaries, dynamic linking, code signing, segments, sections, and symbol tables.

documents.title: Obfuscation
documents.summary: Obfuscation transforms code and data to make analysis harder while preserving behavior. It may involve instruction substitution, control-flow flattening, string encryption, and junk code insertion.

documents.title: Packers
documents.summary: Packers compress or encrypt executable code and data, then wrap it in a small loader stub. At runtime, the stub decompresses or decrypts the original payload in memory before transferring control.

documents.title: Program Database (PDB)
documents.summary: Program Database (PDB) files store debugging symbols, type information, and source mapping for Windows binaries. They connect compiled code to high-level symbols, source files, line numbers, and variable metadata.

documents.title: Portable Executable (PE)
documents.summary: The Portable Executable (PE) format is the canonical Windows binary container for executables, DLLs, drivers, and system libraries. It defines the on-disk layout, loader metadata, runtime section mapping, import/export resolution, relocation data, thread-local storage, resources, and debug information.

documents.title: Protectors
documents.summary: Protectors are advanced wrappers that combine packing with integrity checks, anti-debugging, anti-tampering, and virtualized code paths. They increase the difficulty of reverse engineering and often serve as DRM or malware obfuscation.

documents.title: Relocations
documents.summary: Relocations are the runtime patching mechanism that allows a PE image to execute correctly when it is not loaded at its preferred base address. They are essential for ASLR support, manual mapping, injection, and reliability across different processes.

documents.title: Resource Sections
documents.summary: PE resource sections store application assets, configuration data, and UI elements. Resources are organized as a hierarchical tree keyed by type, name, and language.

documents.title: Symbols
documents.summary: Symbols connect addresses to human-readable identifiers. They are critical for debuggers, reverse engineering tools, and symbol recovery workflows. Symbols may represent: - exported functions and data - imported APIs - local functions, labels, and variables - type metadata and debug information

documents.title: Thread Local Storage (TLS)
documents.summary: Thread Local Storage (TLS) in PE binaries reserves thread-private data and provides an early execution vector through callbacks. The Windows loader initializes TLS data before thread execution begins, and callbacks are invoked during process startup and thread creation.

documents.title: Encrypted String Pattern
documents.summary: Encrypted string patterns describe code that stores plaintext strings in encoded or encrypted form and reconstructs them at runtime. This is common in protectors and malware to hide command-and-control URLs, error messages, and API names.

documents.title: Export Table Pattern
documents.summary: The export table pattern identifies how a module exposes functions and data to external callers. Exported symbols are often the easiest externally observable behavior of a DLL or executable.

documents.title: Import Table Pattern
documents.summary: The import table pattern identifies how a PE binary depends on external libraries and APIs. The import table is also a common target for packers and protectors, which may obfuscate or reconstruct imports at runtime.

documents.title: Obfuscated Jump Pattern
documents.summary: Obfuscated jumps disrupt normal control flow by using indirect branches, opaque predicates, and dispatcher loops. This pattern is common in binary protectors, virtualized code, and obfuscated logic.

documents.title: Packer Pattern
documents.summary: Packer patterns identify binaries that use runtime compression, encryption, and loader stubs. Key indicators include: - unusually large code section containing a small loader stub - anonymous or padded sections like `.UPX`, `.aspack`, `.themida` - relocated import tables or import name hashing - a minimal entry point that resolves imports and then jumps to a second stage - presence of TLS callbacks used to start unpacking

documents.title: Switch Table Pattern
documents.summary: Switch tables implement multi-way branching using jump tables. They appear in compiler-generated code for `switch` statements and are often reused by obfuscation techniques.

documents.title: Themida Pattern
documents.summary: Themida is a commercial protector that combines obfuscation, virtualization, anti-debugging, and packer-style loader behavior.

documents.title: TLS Callback Pattern
documents.summary: TLS callbacks are functions registered in the PE TLS directory and executed by the loader before the normal entry point. They are a common technique in packers and protectors to run initialization and unpacking logic early.

documents.title: UPX Pattern
documents.summary: UPX is a popular packer that compresses executables and wraps them with a small decompression stub. UPX-packed binaries are widely encountered in malware and legitimate software.

documents.title: VMProtect Pattern
documents.summary: VMProtect is a protector that virtualizes code paths and combines packing with anti-debugging. It is commonly used to protect sensitive algorithms and DRM logic.

documents.title: CPU Architecture
documents.summary: The CPU is the central execution engine of a computer system. It interprets instructions, performs arithmetic and logic operations, manages control flow, and coordinates access to memory and I/O. Modern CPUs are built from multiple cores, execution units, caches, and control logic that together enable high instruction throughput and low latency.

documents.title: Asymmetric Cryptography Knowledge
documents.summary: - Prefer established libraries and avoid implementing crypto primitives yourself. - Use strong curves (e.g., Curve25519, P-256) and recommend key sizes per current guidelines.

documents.title: Certificates and PKI Knowledge
documents.summary: - Always validate full certificate chains and consider OCSP stapling or CRL checks for critical paths. - Verify certificate extensions and intended key usages before trusting a certificate for a purpose.

documents.title: Cryptography Fundamentals

documents.title: Hashing and Integrity Knowledge
documents.summary: - Use modern hash algorithms (SHA-256+) and prefer HMAC/AEAD constructions for authenticated integrity. - For password storage, use memory-hard KDFs (Argon2) and include salts per user.

documents.title: Symmetric Encryption Knowledge
documents.summary: - OpenSSL: `openssl enc -aes-256-gcm` and libsodium examples for AEAD encryption testing. - Use test vectors from NIST or RFCs to confirm algorithm correctness.

documents.title: Anti-Debugging Knowledge
documents.summary: Anti-debugging refers to techniques used by software to detect or interfere with debuggers. Debuggers must account for these protections when analyzing crashes, behavior, or reverse-engineering protected binaries.

documents.title: Debugging Knowledge
documents.summary: Debugging is the process of examining runtime state, control flow, and memory to diagnose faults, hangs, or unexpected behavior in native software. In Windows, debugging covers user-mode applications, games, kernel drivers, crash dumps, and protected or anti-debug-aware binaries.

documents.title: Kernel Debugging Knowledge
documents.summary: Kernel debugging is the process of inspecting Windows kernel and driver state using a debugger attached to the operating system rather than a single user-mode process. It is essential for diagnosing blue screens, driver faults, deadlocks, and system corruption.

documents.title: Debug Process Prompt
documents.summary: You are a debugging expert. Given a native Windows user-mode or kernel-mode target, identify the best instrumentation points, symbol requirements, and debugger commands needed to diagnose the fault or hang. Recommend a workflow for collecting dump files, analyzing call stacks, and verifying root cause.

documents.title: Exception Analysis Prompt
documents.summary: You are a debugging expert. Given a crash dump or exception event, identify the exception cause, handler path, and any runtime protections affecting the fault. Recommend commands to inspect the exception record, stack, registers, and symbol state.

documents.title: Hang Analysis Prompt
documents.summary: You are a debugging expert. Given a hung process or hang dump, identify the thread and synchronization state responsible for the freeze. Recommend commands to inspect waiting objects, thread stacks, and blocked resources.

documents.title: Handle Tables Knowledge
documents.summary: - Use debugger extensions (`!handle`, `!object`) to translate handle values to kernel object addresses and inspect object headers. - Correlate handle access masks with requested operations to diagnose access-denied behaviors.

documents.title: Interrupts and System Calls Knowledge

documents.title: IRQL, APC, and DPC Knowledge
documents.summary: - Always validate IRQL assumptions before invoking synchronization or kernel services that may block. - Use dedicated tests that exercise APC and DPC paths under load to reveal timing-dependent bugs.

documents.title: Object Manager and Memory Manager Knowledge
documents.summary: - When investigating object lifetimes, inspect `OBJECT_HEADER` and associated `NAME_INFO` blocks to confirm naming and ownership. - For memory analysis, prefer walking VADs before inspecting page tables to understand allocation intent.

documents.title: Process and Thread Manager Knowledge
documents.summary: - WinDbg: `!process`, `!thread`, and `!handle` to inspect process/thread state and handle tables. - Use live tracing (ETW) to capture thread life-cycle events across process boundaries.

documents.title: Scheduler and Dispatcher Knowledge

documents.title: Reverse Engineering Workflow
documents.summary: > A systematic methodology for analyzing unknown Windows binaries. Follow these phases in order. Skip nothing on first pass — evidence from any phase informs all others.

documents.title: Common Instruction Idioms (x86/x64)
documents.summary: This reference collects common assembly idioms and small patterns that frequently appear during reverse engineering of x86 and x64 binaries. Use these patterns to speed identification of constructs such as vtables, thunks, calling convention boundaries, and compiler-generated helpers.

documents.title: Compiler and Optimization Patterns
documents.summary: Compiler optimizations and ABI shims introduce recognizable instruction patterns that can be mistaken for hand-written logic. This page outlines common compiler-generated constructs, how to detect them, and how to treat them during reverse engineering.

documents.title: C++ Exceptions and SEH
documents.summary: C++ exceptions (language-level) and Structured Exception Handling (SEH) are distinct but interoperable on Windows. Understanding unwind metadata and exception handling is important for control-flow recovery.

documents.title: PDB (Program Database)
documents.summary: PDB files store debug symbols, type information, and source-file mappings. They are invaluable for recovering names, function prototypes, and class layouts when available.

documents.title: STL Containers (Layouts and Common Patterns)
documents.summary: Common STL containers encountered in binaries: `std::vector`, `std::string`, `std::list`, `std::map`.

documents.title: Il2Cpp / Unity Notes
documents.summary: This document explains practical steps for reversing Unity games that use IL2CPP (C# -> C++ conversion) and provides guidance for extracting metadata, recovering method bindings, and generating usable SDKs.

documents.title: Source Engine Notes
documents.summary: Notes and practical patterns for reversing games built on Valve's Source engine. Focuses on interface discovery, object layouts, and common engine conventions used by mods and plugins.

documents.title: Windows Loader
documents.summary: The Windows loader is responsible for preparing a PE image to execute: mapping sections, resolving imports, applying relocations, initializing TLS and running static constructors. Understanding the loader is essential when recovering runtime behavior or performing in-memory analysis.

documents.title: Memory Manager and Virtual Memory
documents.summary: Windows virtual memory is a foundational topic for both static and dynamic reverse engineering. This page summarizes essential concepts, practical diagnostics, and common pitfalls when working with process memory.

documents.title: ntdll internals
documents.summary: `ntdll.dll` is the primary user-mode interface for Native API (Nt* calls) and contains syscall stubs, runtime helpers, and structures used across Windows.

documents.title: Object Manager (Handles and Kernel Objects)
documents.summary: The Object Manager is a kernel component that manages named kernel objects and handle tables. In user-mode RE you will mostly interact via handles returned by Win32 API, but understanding object types and semantics helps with behavioral analysis.

documents.title: PEB (Process Environment Block)
documents.summary: The Process Environment Block (PEB) is a user-mode structure that contains process-wide information used by the Windows loader and runtime. The PEB is useful in reverse engineering for finding loaded modules, process parameters, and loader data structures.

documents.title: Syscalls and Native API
documents.summary: Syscalls are the boundary into kernel mode. On x64 Windows, user-mode `ntdll` stubs typically use the `syscall` instruction with a syscall number in `rax`.

documents.title: TEB (Thread Environment Block)
documents.summary: The Thread Environment Block (TEB) stores thread-local information such as exception lists, thread-local storage (TLS), stack limits, and a pointer to the owning thread's PEB. The TEB is useful for locating per-thread data and thread-specific state during dynamic analysis.

documents.title: Calling Conventions (Windows x86/x64)
documents.summary: This document summarizes common calling conventions encountered on Windows and provides practical notes for recognizing argument passing, callee/caller-saved registers, and differences between architectures.

documents.title: Registers
documents.summary: Reference for common register roles and practical tips for Windows analysis (x86/x64).

documents.title: C++ ABI (MSVC)
documents.summary: Overview of Microsoft C++ ABI details relevant to reverse engineering and type reconstruction on Windows targets.

documents.title: C++ RTTI in MSVC (Run-Time Type Information)
documents.summary: RTTI allows programs to determine the type of an object at runtime. MSVC implements RTTI through a set of structures embedded in the binary, primarily used by `dynamic_cast` and `typeid`. Understanding these structures is essential for recovering class names and hierarchies from binaries.

documents.title: C++ Virtual Function Tables (vtables)
documents.summary: A **vtable** (virtual method table) is a mechanism used by C++ compilers to implement runtime polymorphism. Every class with at least one virtual function — or that inherits from such a class — gets a vtable. Each *instance* of such a class carries a hidden pointer (the **vptr**) to its class's vtable.

documents.title: Unity / Il2Cpp Notes
documents.summary: Practical guide for working with Unity games that use IL2CPP. Focuses on metadata extraction, type reconstruction, and generating usable SDKs for analysis and tooling.

documents.title: Unreal Engine Notes
documents.summary: Guidance for reversing games built on Unreal Engine: locating core globals (`GWorld`, `GNames`), understanding `UObject` layout, and identifying `ProcessEvent` and reflection hooks.

documents.title: Prompt: Analyze Binary
documents.summary: Perform a complete initial analysis of an unknown Windows PE binary. Produce a structured report covering file type, packing status, capabilities, imported APIs, identified classes, key algorithms, and entry points for deeper analysis. This is the triage phase — the goal is maximum information density in minimum time.

documents.title: Prompt: Analyze Crash
documents.summary: Determine the root cause of a crash in a Windows native process. Identify the faulting instruction, the bad memory access, the call path leading to the fault, and the original code defect. Produce an actionable root cause report.

documents.title: Prompt: Analyze Memory
documents.summary: Enumerate and log all such regions. If size > 4KB and not in PEB module list → potential injection.

documents.title: Prompt: Build ReClass.NET Layout
documents.summary: | IDA type | ReClass node type | Notes | |-------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------| | `QWORD` pointer | Pointer (8 bytes) | Set child class if known | | `DWORD` int | Int32 | | | `float` | Float | | | `bool` (byte) | Bool / Int8 | | | `HANDLE` (qword) | Hex64 | Show as hex | | `char[N]` array | UTF8Text or Bytes | | | `wchar_t[N]` | UTF16Text | | | embedded object | Class node (inline) | Expand the embedded struct | | `void*` unknown | Pointer → Unknown | Placeholder | | padding bytes | Padding (N bytes) | |

documents.title: Prompt: Identify Game Engine
documents.summary: **Unreal Engine 4/5:** ``` "LogInit:", "LogEngine:", "UObject", "UWorld", "AActor" "GEngine", "GWorld", "UGameInstance" "Unreal Engine", "Epic Games", ".uasset", ".pak" RTTI: .?AVUObject@@, .?AVAActor@@, .?AVUWorld@@ ```

documents.title: Prompt: Pattern Scan
documents.summary: Create, validate, and deploy a byte-pattern signature for a target function or data structure. The pattern must be version-stable, unique within the binary, and robust against minor updates.

documents.title: Prompt: Recover C++ Class Layout
documents.summary: Reconstruct a complete C++ class definition from a stripped MSVC binary. Produce an accurate struct layout with all field offsets, types, sizes, and names. The output must be importable into ReClass.NET and usable as an IDA struct type.

documents.title: Prompt: Recover Offsets
documents.summary: Systematically locate and document all stable memory offsets for a target binary: struct field offsets, pointer chain paths, global variable RVAs, vtable slot indices, and function RVAs. Produce a version-tagged offset table suitable for production use.

documents.title: Prompt: Recover RTTI Structures
documents.summary: Extract all C++ class names and inheritance relationships from a stripped MSVC binary using Run-Time Type Information (RTTI). Produce a complete class hierarchy map with vtable addresses, class names, and base class relationships.

documents.title: Prompt: Recover Vtable
documents.summary: Locate, validate, and fully document all vtables in an MSVC binary. For each vtable: identify the owning class, enumerate all virtual function slots with names and signatures, detect thunks, and produce a complete vtable definition for IDA structs and ReClass.

documents.title: Prompt: Rename Classes
documents.summary: Assign accurate, consistent names to all recovered C++ classes in a stripped MSVC binary. Build a class naming table that covers all identified vtables, with demangled names, aliases, and confidence ratings.

documents.title: Prompt: Rename Functions
documents.summary: Systematically rename all unnamed functions in a stripped binary with high-confidence, evidence-based names. Apply consistent naming conventions. Document the evidence for every rename.

documents.title: Protected Process Knowledge

documents.title: Code Integrity Analysis Prompt

documents.title: Windows Mitigations Analysis Prompt
